result.markdown:
--- title: "Growing Up Bilingual" author: "Robert Esposito & Yhosep Barba" institute: "Rutgers University" date: "Last update: 2026-04-15" format: rutgers-revealjs engine: knitr execute: out.width: "100%" fig.asp: 0.5625 dpi: 300 --- # A bit about us {.transition} ##
**[Who are we?]{.emph}**
- We are RU Bilingual - Graduate students in the Spanish & Portuguese department at Rutgers - We study bilingualism and second language acquisition ##
**[What do we do?]{.emph}**
- We want our day-to-day research to be useful to a broader public - We want to create a safe place to discuss multilingualism in our communities ##
**[Our objectives:]{.emph}**
- Support and defend bilingualism at home, in school, and in our community - Allow for academic research to have more practical applications # What we'll be talking about today {.transition} ::: incremental - What is bilingualism? - Linguistic Attitudes & Language Ideologies - What myths exist about bilingualism? - What advantages are there to bilingualism? - How can we contribute to maintaining bilingualism? ::: # What is Bilingualism? {.transition} Do you consider yourself bilingual? ::: notes Discuss in pairs, then share. ::: ## Bilingualism - The ability to understand and/or communicate in more than one language - Is there a minimum requirement to be considered bilingual? ::: fragment Four components:
read / write / speak / listen
::: ## Bilingualism versus Biliteracy - [Biliteracy]{.emph}: ability to read and write in two languages - A [bilingual person]{.emph} is not necessarily biliterate - Some bilinguals [cannot read/write]{.emph} in their two languages ::: fragment - [Less than half]{.emph} of the world's languages today are written [@sil_orthography_development] - About [12%]{.emph} of the human population is illiterate today [@unesco2025factsheet] ::: ::: notes Mention that for the vast majority of history, humans did not write. Today, the vast majority of languages remain solely oral. ::: ## Bilingualism in the world ::: incremental - How many languages are there in the world? - More than 6,900! - How many countries are there? - 206 ::: ::: fragment More than [33 languages per country]{.emph} The truth: [More than half]{.emph} of children around the world grow up in a [bilingual environment]{.emph} ::: ::: notes Many languages are moribund, and we have seen a major extinction event of languages due to colonialism. ::: ## ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="80%"} ![](assets/bilingualism_usa.bmp) ::: ::: {.column width="20%"} **[22%]{.emph}** of people in the U.S. speak a language other than English ::: ::: ## Does that seem tiny? ::: columns ::: column ::: fragment ![](assets/bilingual-chart-without-US.png){width="100%"} ::: ::: ::: column ::: fragment ![](assets/bilingual-chart-with-US.png){width="100%"} ::: ::: ::: ::: notes So we're right at the average percentage compared to the EU. ::: ## Does that seem tiny? ::: columns ::: column ::: fragment ![](assets/number-of-bilinguals-without-US.png) ::: ::: ::: column ::: fragment ![](assets/number-of-bilinguals-with-US.png) ::: ::: ::: ::: notes but we have a TON more speakers! ::: ## ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="70%"} ![](assets/bilingualism_nj.bmp) ::: ::: {.column width="30%"} **[34%]{.emph}** of people in New Jersey speak a language other than English ::: ::: ## ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="80%"} ![](assets/somerset.bmp) ::: ::: {.column width="20%"} **[37%]{.emph}** of people in Somerset County speak a language other than English ::: ::: ::: notes factoid source: https://www.census.gov/acs/www/about/why-we-ask-each-question/language/ map sources for usa & nj: https://lang-map.mla.org/?_ga=2.189842600.266919070.1776158169-1032903345.1776158169&l=Total_Lower_Undergraduate_Enrollments!%2CTotal_Upper_Undergraduate_Enrollments!%2CTotal_Undergraduate_Enrollments!%2CTotal_Graduate_Enrollments!%2CTotal_Institutional_Enrollments!%2CPredominantPrimaryLesson%5B20%5D!%2Cpredominant_language_spoken_excl_english%5B20%5D!%2Cpredominant_language_spoken_excl_spanish_eng%5B20%5D~%2Call_languages_other_engl_pct%2Cother_unspecified_languages_pct!%2Cafrican_languages_pct!%2Carabic_pct!%2Carmenian_pct!%2Cchinese_pct!%2Cfrench_pct!%2Cfrench_creole_pct!%2Cgerman_pct!%2Cgreek_pct!%2Cgujarathi_pct!%2Chebrew_pct!%2Chindi_pct!%2Chmong_pct!%2Chungarian_pct!%2Citalian_pct!%2Cjapanese_pct!%2Ckorean_pct!%2Claotian_pct!%2Cmonkmer_pct!%2Cnavajo_pct!%2Cpersian_pct!%2Cpolish_pct!%2Cportugese_pct!%2Crussian_pct!%2Cscandanavian_pct!%2Cserbian_croatian_pct!%2Cspanish_pct!%2Ctagalog_pct!%2Cthai_pct!%2Curdu_pct!%2Cvietnamese_pct!%2Cyiddish_pct!%2Cother_asian_pct!%2Cother_indic_pct!%2Cother_indoeuropean_pct!%2Cother_pacificisland_pct!%2Cother_slavic_pct!%2Cother_naindigenous_pct!%2Cother_westgermanic_pct!%2Call_languages_other_engl_cnt!%2Cother_unspecified_languages_cnt!%2Cafrican_languages_cnt!%2Carabic_cnt!%2Carmenian_cnt!%2Cchinese_cnt!%2Cfrench_cnt!%2Cfrench_creole_cnt!%2Cgerman_cnt!%2Cgreek_cnt!%2Cgujarathi_cnt!%2Chebrew_cnt!%2Chindi_cnt!%2Chmong_cnt!%2Chungarian_cnt!%2Citalian_cnt!%2Cjapanese_cnt!%2Ckorean_cnt!%2Claotian_cnt!%2Cmonkmer_cnt!%2Cnavajo_cnt!%2Cpersian_cnt!%2Cpolish_cnt!%2Cportugese_cnt!%2Crussian_cnt!%2Cscandanavian_cnt!%2Cserbian_croatian_cnt!%2Cspanish_cnt!%2Ctagalog_cnt!%2Cthai_cnt!%2Curdu_cnt!%2Cvietnamese_cnt!%2Cyiddish_cnt!%2Cother_asian_cnt!%2Cother_indic_cnt!%2Cother_indoeuropean_cnt!%2Cother_pacificisland_cnt!%2Cother_slavic_cnt!%2Cother_naindigenous_cnt!%2Cother_westgermanic_cnt!&bl=mapnik&t=qgis_mapserv.fcgi&c=-10710812%2C4732693&s=20000000 map source for somerset county: https://experience.arcgis.com/experience/cbd39e908efc459abbc2aa51916aed09/page/Language-Spoken-At-Home ::: ## Summing it up... ::: incremental - In our part of the world, about 1 in 3 people are bilingual - We see that in the US, bilingualism is particularly concentrated in specific areas - The New York/New Jersey areas is one! - We want to [support]{.emph} our bilingual population by providing... - adequate [language access]{.emph} resources - adequate [education]{.emph} ::: # Linguistic Attitudes & Language Ideologies {.transition} ## Despite the [large]{.emph} number of bilinguals in the world, and especially in the United States, people still have [very different reactions]{.emph} to bilingualism. ## Let's reflect... 1. Is one language "better" than another? Why? 2. Which language sounds more romantic? Where does that idea come from? 3. Which variety of Spanish is "better"? Mexican? Colombian? Another? 4. How do young people speak today? 5. Do you think your parents thought the same way at your age? 6. What assumptions do we make about how wealthy people speak? 7. What about people with less money? ::: notes discuss with a partner ::: ## {.smaller} ::: incremental - [Language attitudes]{.emph} are reactions toward languages, their use in society, and their speakers - These attitudes can influence how we speak - "Language" here variably refers to... - A language (e.g., Spanish) - A variety (e.g., Mexican Spanish) - A style (e.g., youth speech) - A sociolect (e.g., middle-class speech) - Attitudes can be: - Positive - Negative - Neutral ::: ## ::: incremental Three common ways of evaluating language - Correctness → Correct vs. Incorrect - Appropriateness → Better vs. Worse - Aesthetics → Pleasant vs. Ugly ::: ::: fragment But... ::: ::: fragment [Who]{.emph} decides what is correct, better, or pleasant? ::: ## {.smaller} ::: fragment - Linguistic Attitudes - Evaluations or reactions toward languages - Expressed as opinions, beliefs, or prejudices - Individual level ::: ::: fragment - Language Ideologies - Socially constructed ideas about how language should function - Linked to political, social, and economic interests - Shared across groups or societies ::: ::: fragment - Some research - @callesano2019latinx: perceptions of Spanish varieties - @sima2017actitudes: pride and identity in Yucatec Maya - @purnell1999perceptual: linguistic profiling ::: ##

"Contemporary raciolinguistic ideologies must be situated within colonial histories that shaped the co-naturalization of language and race as part of the project of modernity" [@rosa2017unsettling] ## What do [colonial processes]{.emph} do? ::: fragment - They [systematize]{.emph} and [classify]{.emph} language and language use: ::: ::: fragment - Appropriate vs. inappropriate - Intelligent vs. unintelligent - e.g.: Grammar of the Castilian Language [@nebrija1492gramatica] ::: ## Raciolinguistic ideologies in early colonization - Applied to Indigenous populations - Language used to justify hierarchies and difference - Today: the “white listening subject” ## Raciolinguistic ideologies in early colonization - Certain ways of speaking are evaluated through white, normative expectations - Raciolinguistics today: - Stigmatization of Latinx English and Spanish in the U.S. - Stigmatization of dialects that have been previously racialized ## Language attitudes and ideologies also shape the concept of bilingualism - [Monoglossic ideologies]{.emph} - "One nation, one language" - shapes educational policies - The reification of the "native speaker" ## Who counts as a "native speaker"? Research shows this figure is often imagined as: - White - Middle or upper class - From specific regions ::: fragment
These [assumptions]{.emph} lead to [misdiagnosis]{.emph} and [misplacement]{.emph} of students in educational systems.
::: ## We are often told that being bilingual is an [advantage]{.emph} in society ::: fragment ... but an advantage for who? ::: ::: fragment @zavala2024ideology argue that bilingualism is not a neutral category, but [political]{.emph} ::: ## ::: incremental - In the United States, knowing an indigenous language like [Nahuatl]{.emph} has been seen as [useless]{.emph} - In many countries, indigenous languages are seen as [lesser]{.emph} because they do not have a standard grammar or a written form - In Peru, "bilingual" is often used with [deficit connotations]{.emph} for Quechua-Spanish bilinguals - In the Philippines, English-Filipino bilingualism [erased]{.emph} the presence of more than 100 local languages. ::: ## So we should ask: ::: fragment - What counts as bilingual in your context? - Only English and Spanish? - What about indigenous languages? ::: ::: fragment
Bilingualism is not fixed. It is a diverse and situated experience.
::: # What myths exist about bilingualism? {.transition} ## True or false? 1. Being bilingual is like being two monolingual people in one body. 2. You have to have proficient skills in speaking, reading, writing, and listening to be considered bilingual. 3. Bilingual children can't achieve/gain the same knowledge that monolingual children can. 4. Using two languages at once means you aren't proficient in either. ::: notes Discuss with a partner ::: ## Being bilingual is like being two monolingual people in one body. ::: fragment
[FALSE]{.p-font style="font-size: 3em"}
::: ::: fragment - There is no strict separation between a bilingual's two languages in their brain - It is like a spider web: when you touch a string, the whole web moves ::: ## Being bilingual is like being two monolingual people in one body. - A bilingual's two languages are always "on" [@paradis1993linguistic] - This is important when we want to speak and read in one language ![](assets/whackamole.jpg) ::: notes Nuestros idiomas son como los topitos de Whack a mole, están siempre intentado salir y nosotros tenemos que aplastarlos para que nos dejen hablar el idioma que queremos en el momento que queremos. ::: ## Being bilingual is like being two monolingual people in one body. - A multilingual speaker's languages are always competing for activation - Activation can depend on... - semantics - phonetics - syntax ## Being bilingual is like being two monolingual people in one body.
Voy a ir hasta la iglesia en...

::: fragment - Semantic competitors: - carro, coche, automóvil, bus, taxi, Uber, Lyft, car, automobile, bus, taxi... ::: ::: fragment - Phonetic competitors: - [ca]{.emph}rro, [ca]{.emph}r, [ca]{.emph}sa, [ca]{.emph}ja, [ca]{.emph}ma, [ca]{.emph}rton, [ca]{.emph}rver, [ca]{.emph}rgo... ::: ## You have to have proficient skills in speaking, reading, writing, and listening to be considered bilingual. ::: fragment
[FALSE]{.p-font style="font-size: 3em"}
::: ## You have to have proficient skills in speaking, reading, writing, and listening to be considered bilingual. - Bilingualism comes in all shapes and sizes. - People who study dead languages like Latin or Greek might only be able to [read]{.emph}. - Many languages do not have written forms. ## You have to have proficient skills in speaking, reading, writing, and listening to be considered bilingual. Many people who grow up in the US are exposed to a language other than English at home, and there are many outcomes: - can only comprehend spoken language - can speak and comprehend, but cannot read or write - can read, write, speak, and comprehend spoken language ## Bilingual children can't achieve/gain the same knowledge that monolingual children can. ::: fragment
[FALSE]{.p-font style="font-size: 3em"}
::: ## Bilingual children can't achieve/gain the same knowledge that monolingual children can. ::: incremental - Children are evaluated in phases, typically on a standardized scale - e.g., by 2 years old, a child should produce at least 200 words - [Problem]{.emph}: the same scale is normally used to evaluate these two different populations ::: ## Bilingual children can't achieve/gain the same knowledge that monolingual children can. ::: incremental - Bilingual children spread their knowledge [across two languages]{.emph} - They know 200 total words, but only 100 in English - They know how to count in English, but not in Spanish - This leads to [over-diagnosis]{.emph} of developmental delays, when really they may just need a bit more language support at school! ::: ## Using two languages at once means you aren't proficient in either. ::: fragment
[FALSE]{.p-font style="font-size: 3em"}
::: ::: fragment - Switching between two languages in a conversation requires [proficiency]{.emph} in both languages [@gumperz1999codeswitching] ::: ## Using two languages at once means you aren't proficient in either. - Bilingual children know what language to speak with who [@harrison1980young] - Mixing languages is not a sign of confusion or laziness - There are [rules]{.emph} to mixing languages -- it's not random, and the child knows them! - We call this language-mixing [codeswitching]{.emph} ::: notes La comprensión y la producción de un bilingüe es diferente de la producción y la comprensión de un monolingüe porque los idiomas están siempre encendidos. ::: ## Mixing languages Rate these sentences on a scale of 1 (very unnatural) to 5 (very natural): - Can you peinarme? - Fui a the tienda. - Busco for a pair of shoes. - I am estrenando this t-shirt. - El plato está en el sink. - I bought a rojo car. ## Codeswitching and Spanglish
"Es una combinación de fenómenos de contacto distintos. Estos fenómenos incluyen préstamos, calcos, extensiones semánticas, préstamos temporales, mezclas, entre otros" [@casielles2017spanglish]
- troca ~ truck - llamar pa'trás ~ to call back - carpeta ~ carpet - introducir ~ introduce
"Spanglish is what we speak, who we Latinos are, and how we act and how we perceive the world" [@morales2007living]
## Codeswitching - Estás ready? - Vamos a janguear. - Vamos para el mall. "Si quieres empezar otra pelea, I'm not in the mood. Anyway, vine a otra cosa" [@prida1991beautiful]
Do you have other examples?
## Spanglish in the Wild ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} ![](assets/spanglish1.png){width="60%"} ![](assets/spanglish2.png){width="60%"} ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ![](assets/spanglish3.png){width="70%"} ![](assets/spanglish4.png){width="60%"} ::: ::: # Are There Advantages to Being Bilingual? {.transition} ## Let's do an experiment... Say the color of the [ink]{.emph}. Do not read the text. For example, if you see:
[blue]{.emph}
you say **red**. ##



[Ready?]{.p-font style="font-size: 5em"}
##



[brown]{.p-font style="color: red; font-size: 5em"}
##



[yellow]{.p-font style="color: blue; font-size: 5em"}
##



[red]{.p-font style="color: yellow; font-size: 5em"}
##



[brown]{.p-font style="color: green; font-size: 5em"}
##



[pink]{.p-font style="color: black; font-size: 5em"}
##



[green]{.p-font style="color: orange; font-size: 5em"}
## The Stroop Test We use this task to test [executive function]{.emph}, which involves... ::: incremental - Self control - Working memory - Mental flexibility ::: ::: notes not acting on impulse. holding information in mind. switching between tasks/perspectives. ::: ## Executive Function in Bilinguals - It's been found that bilinguals have greater [executive function]{.emph} compared to monolinguals [@coderre2014electrophysiological] ::: incremental - This means that bilinguals tend to... - have better impulse control - hold more information in their mind in the short-term - have greater ease switching between tasks ::: ## What if my child has Autism or ADHD? - Some parents are concerned that hearing or speaking more than one language may [confuse]{.emph} a child

What do you think?
## What if my child has Autism or ADHD? ::: incremental - For a long time, being bilingual was seen as a [disadvantage]{.emph} - Parents were told to speak [only English]{.emph} at home, and many teachers still suggest this! - This is especially the case for parents of children with autism or ADHD ::: ## Bilingualism and ADHD - People diagnosed with attention deficit disorders tend to have [poorer]{.emph} executive function - However, bilingualism seems to [mitigate]{.emph} the impact when under cognitive duress [@brouillardchallenges] ## Bilingualism and Autism ::: incremental - [Autism]{.emph} is an umbrella term for a spectrum of differences from the [neurotypical]{.emph} population - A typical fear is that a child with autism will be burdened by hearing and speaking two languages - However, we have evidence in the contrary! - Bilingual children with autism do not show any delays greater than their monolingual counterparts - In fact, children with autism from a bilingual environment who are raised as monolingual show detrimental effects! ::: ::: footer @beauchamp2017bilingualism ::: ## Bilingualism and the Brain - Bilingualism makes your brain healthier! - There is strong evidence that bilinguals retain [more brain matter]{.emph} as they grow older compared to monolinguals [@heim2019bilingualism] - There is also evidence that bilingualism [delays the onset of Alzheimer's disease]{.emph}! [@woumans2015bilingualism] ## In sum... ::: incremental - Bilingualism does not delay development in typical or atypical populations - It is [healthy]{.emph} for the brain and provides certain [advantage]{.emph} - Provides greater [executive function]{.emph} ::: ::: fragment - better short-term memory - greater multitasking skills - more self control ::: # How Can we Contribute to Maintaining Bilingualism? {.transition} ## What can you do to maintain your language(s)? ::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} ![](assets/family1.jpg) ![](assets/family2.png) ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ![](assets/family3.png) ![](assets/family4.png) ::: ::: ::: notes Do activities with your family. Language is social. ::: ## What can you do to maintain your language(s)? ::: incremental - The language(s) should be spoken whenever possible - talk about daily activities - tell stories and histories - ask questions - motivate everyone to participate ::: ::: fragment
[USE YOUR LANGAUGE!]{.emph}
::: ## What can you do to maintain your language(s)? Create a [network]{.emph} of speakers - Look for community activities and people outside your home - Don't be afraid of using different languages at home - Interact with others in your target language ## What can you do to maintain your language(s)? - Tell or read stories in the target language - Listening is important too! - Promote reading and writing skills by teaching or actively modeling these skills ## What can you do to maintain your language(s)? - Create safe place for people to communicate in their languages ![](assets/zimmerli.png) ## Thanks! {.final visibility="uncounted"} ::: {.absolute top="20" left="0"}
{{< qrcode https://www.instagram.com/ru_bilingual/ width=150 height=150 colorDark='#cc0033' >}}

\@ru_bilingual

::: ![](assets/dot_front.png){.absolute top="0" right="0" width="200" height="200"} ::: {.absolute top="300" left="0"}

include-after-body:
<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
